title: Array destructuring
instruction:
Instead of accessing array elements by index one by one, destructure them into named variables in one line:

```js
const [first, second, third] = [10, 20, 30];
```

Run this. See how much cleaner it is than `arr[0]`, `arr[1]`, `arr[2]`.
successMessage: [r, g, b] = rgb — cleaner than rgb[0], rgb[1], rgb[2]. The ...rest captures everything that wasn't explicitly named.

title: Skip elements
instruction:
Use commas to skip elements you don't need:

```js
const [, second, , fourth] = [1, 2, 3, 4];
```

Extract only the first and last items from the array.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Commas skip elements. [first, , , , last] — four commas to skip three elements. Especially useful when a function returns an array and you only need specific positions.

title: Object destructuring
hint: const { name, age, city, email } = user;

codeChecks.message: Destructure: const { name, age, city } = user;
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: name, age, city — three variables in one line. This is how React components receive props: function Component({ name, age, onClick }) — all destructured from the props object.

title: Rename while destructuring
hint: const { name: userA } = user1; const { name: userB } = user2;

codeChecks.message: Rename user2's name: { name: nameB, score: scoreB } = user2
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Renaming during destructuring is clean and explicit. { originalName: newVariable } — the property name on the left, your variable name on the right.

title: Default values in destructuring
instruction:
Set defaults for missing properties — they kick in when the property is undefined:

```js
const { name, role = 'user', theme = 'dark' } = preferences;
```

Destructure the config with sensible defaults.
hint: const { host = 'localhost', port = 3000, debug = false } = config;
successMessage: port defaulted to 3000, debug to false — because they weren't in config. Defaults + destructuring is the cleanest way to handle optional function parameters.

title: Nested destructuring
instruction:
Destructure nested objects and arrays inline:

```js
const { address: { city, country } } = user;
```

Extract the nested address data.
hint: const { name, address: { city, country } } = user;

codeChecks.message: Use nested destructuring: { address: { city, country } }
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Nested destructuring in one line. API responses are often 5-10 levels deep — nested destructuring handles them elegantly.

title: Destructuring in function params
hint: function formatOrder({ id, customer: { name }, total, items }) { ... }

codeChecks.message: Destructure in the params: function formatOrder({ id, ... })

successMessage: Params destructuring is everywhere in modern JavaScript. React components: function Button({ label, onClick, disabled = false }) — clean and self-documenting.

title: Spread in function calls
instruction:
Spread `...` works in function calls to expand an array into individual arguments:

```js
Math.max(...numbers);  // instead of Math.max(a, b, c, d)
```

Use spread to find the max, min, and call a multi-arg function.
successMessage: Spread in calls expands the array into arguments. Math.max(85, 92, 67, 98...) written as Math.max(...scores). Works with any function that takes multiple args.

title: Build an API response handler
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Write `handleResponse({ data, error, meta: { total, page } = {} })` that:
- If error is present: prints `Error: {error.message}`
- If data is empty: prints `No results on page {page}`
- Otherwise: prints a summary like `Page {page} of {total}: {data.length} results`

Test with 3 different response shapes.
hint: function handleResponse({ data = [], error, meta: { total = '?', page = 1 } = {} }) { ... }
successMessage: Destructuring with nested objects, defaults, and renaming all in the function signature. This is how real API response handlers look in production. Chapter 11 done!
